# Transcrição: 06- Course (Lesson 2 - Part 4) - Understanding Cap Rates & Debt Coverage

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Okay, let's discuss cap rates and debt coverage ratio. Debt coverage ratio, DCR, may be a term you've never heard. Hopefully you heard it when I shared it 44 times earlier. But even so, it's one of those very important terms in real estate investing. Where a lot of investors think the bank, the debt is like the bad guy. I think the debt, the bank, the lender is my partner. I treat them as a partner that can help me. This DCR, if you understand it, it will keep you from getting in trouble in deals. The DCR is the point at which your debt is covered by NOI. The DCR is the point at which your debt, your financing amount, your principal and interest is covered by the NOI that produces free cash flow. While it sounds fairly basic in a down economy, this can determine again whether or not you lose your property or you manage it and stay afloat. The biggest mistake I made in 2008 was I didn't buy everything in my neighborhood. Everybody that was around me that got in trouble was losing their properties, including banks going under. The bank that I owed money to went under, but I didn't. Some people would tell you to avoid debt entirely, but I'm not one of those people. As a result, you have to understand if you're on my side in my camp, if you like the idea of using debt and leverage, Caterpillar uses it, Coca-Cola uses it. Look, companies that don't even need debt, like Apple Computer, use debt because it's cheaper than using their own money. You need to understand and embrace the DCR concept to make sure that you're always where you need to be. My rule of thumb is 125%. 25% more than the debt I took on needs to be my NOI. If I'm paying $100,000 of principal and interest on my loan, I need the NOI to be 125. I can go lower than that. I could go down to 115, but for short periods of time, if I really believe in a property, maybe it needs to be rehabbed and I can go back in and raise the rents, I might go down as low as 115. But a 125 cover DCR of 125 is a really safe number. This gives me 25% more than my debt requirements to be comfortable, to sleep at night, to not worry. If you don't violate this, you'll never get in trouble. If you do, you'll be veering into choppy waters when the economy changes. Then there's the cap rate. These two things work kind of hand in hand. It's a very important number. Cap rate is one number that you'll hear a lot more than a DCR until you start doing deals.

## [03:00 - 06:04]

The cap rate is thrown around loosely by mostly amateurs who look for a 6 and 8, a 10 cap rate. The moment I hear a 10 cap rate, I run as fast as I can. I know I'm not interested in that property. Remember, the higher the cap rate, the higher the cash on cash. But why would I earn 12 or 14 or 18% on my investment if it was a great deal? If it was a great deal, met life or potential will be bidding against me and be tickled to death with a 5 or 6% return. Any cap rate is the number one question I get when I'm speaking at real estate conferences or to investors. Your capitalization rate, cap rate, this is your NOI divided by the current market value or price you're paying for the property. This number shows you the potential rate of return on your real estate investment if you paid cash. NOI is the number, the potential rate of return on your real estate investment if you paid cash. If it was a $1 million property and an 8% cap rate, that means that property before dead would pay you $80,000 a year. Again, the higher the cap rate indicates a property is a better deal. I don't know if it's a better deal or not. I know it's going to pay them more money per month, but it doesn't mean it's a better exit. It means it's in a worse neighborhood because it pays more. The risk is higher, so it's got to have a higher cap rate. Higher risk, higher cap rate. Lower risk, lower cap rate. Your cap rate that you're trying to sell your property for immediately indicates to me whether you're a real seller and indicates to me what type of property it might be. Would I ever buy just on cap rate? Never. Never buy any property on any one metric. Price will not be the given factor for me. The down payment would never be the given factor for me. The cap rate, no one item. It's the whole package. But the cap rate could get me to lean into the deal absolutely. So if you tell me, hey, Grant, downtown Houston, it's a 4% cap rate, I get an idea of that building immediately because I've done my homework. If you tell me it's a 7% cap rate in the Midwest, I'm going to ask how old it is, how many units and what the location is. So the cap rate won't scare me off low or high, but it's going to force me or help me start asking other questions about why I might be interested in that portfolio or that package. Most real estate investors agree that a 4% cap rate is great, a good cap rate in high demand areas where 10% or higher cap rate would make sense in lower demand, higher unemployment, more difficult tenants possibly areas. The higher the cap rate, the higher the return, the lower the cap rate, the lower the return,

## [06:04 - 09:05]

the higher the cap rate, the more trouble, the lower the cap rate, hopefully the lower the trouble and the more desirable long term. So how does the deal work? I've been giving you the basics. The next step to understand how a deal works is to actually start walking on deals, working deals, looking at deals. You'll have the spot of building that you might be interested in buying or not buying, by the way. There's many deals, many deals that I do do diligence on that I have zero intention of buying. Here's one, two, three. Here's a deal that I spent $25,000 of my money on to determine I wouldn't buy it. I wanted to buy it. I spent $25,000, not to mention my time and energy and staff investing into a deal and didn't buy it. I didn't buy it now. It doesn't mean I won't buy it in a year or two years. So if you're going to play this game, you have to start walking deals and looking at deals, keep files on all your deals. Spot something that you're interested in. Find out if it's on the market. Maybe it's not on the market. Maybe it just sold. Do the due diligence on deals that just sold and track it and see how it performs. Call an agent and get a sense of what's available in the market. What's for sale? What's sold? What does he have that might be coming? If it's formally listed or maybe it's off market and ask, hey, what was the price? There's also software that you can purchase that will tell you what's in your market. What's for sale? What it sold for last time? How much debt they put on it? It's called real capital analytics. It's going to cost you about $25,000 a year just to have that data in real time on what people are paying, what they paid, what their rents are, etc. This is where your marketplace knowledge comes in. By the way, if you don't have the $25,000 to buy the software, put your shoes on and walk properties. I've walked hundreds of thousands of properties. I've made tens of thousands of phone calls and said, send me your deal. You should be looking at deals every day. Even if you have no intention of moving forward with a deal, look at the deal. Do the underwriting. If a deal's overpriced, don't tell them it's overpriced. Do your due diligence, keep your mouth shut, and don't buy the deal. But there's no reason to run a guy's deal down. You have to know what's out there. Every deal in your market you should know about. Understand what it's selling for, what it's renting for, what the occupancy is. Understand how those deals are structured and why. You're going to find out who the real sellers are and who they aren't. By the way, just because I'm not a seller today doesn't mean I'm not a seller tomorrow. If you can, you'll be well positioned. If you make the commitment to doing the homework, you'll be well positioned to make snap assessments
